[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<tbox>
- Class81 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen)
- hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen) sqsubseteq Class81
- Class14 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals)
- hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals) sqsubseteq Class14
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, broad pale pink/white multi-petaled flower)
- hasVisualFeature(Target, prominent central yellow-green stamen)
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
The Target Image has broad, multi-petaled pale pink/white flowers with a prominent central yellow-green stamen, which exactly matches the necessary and sufficient visual features defined for Class81. These features do not align with the bright yellow, narrow-petaled iris shape associated with Class14, so Class81 is the correct label.
</dl_explanation>
<response>81</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

message.content:
<tbox>
- Class81 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen)
- hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen) sqsubseteq Class81
- Class14 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals)
- hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals) sqsubseteq Class14
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, broad pale pink/white multi-petaled flower)
- hasVisualFeature(Target, prominent central yellow-green stamen)
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
The Target Image has broad, multi-petaled pale pink/white flowers with a prominent central yellow-green stamen, which exactly matches the necessary and sufficient visual features defined for Class81. These features do not align with the bright yellow, narrow-petaled iris shape associated with Class14, so Class81 is the correct label.
</dl_explanation>
<response>81</response>
message.reasoning:
Got it, let's work through this step by step. First, the tbox: first look at the examples. Label 81 is the pink/white striped clematis (the first two user examples) and 14 is the yellow iris (the other two). Wait no, the target image is pale pink/white clematis flowers, matching the 81 class? Wait wait no, let's structure the DL properly.

First, TBox: 
1.  Class 81 equiv hasVisualFeature(pink/white layered petals, prominent central stamen, broad flower petals with soft pink/white coloring)
2.  Class 14 equiv hasVisualFeature(bright yellow petals, distinct iris flower shape with long narrow petals)

Then Abox for the target image: hasVisualFeature(Target, soft pale pink and white petals), hasVisualFeature(Target, prominent central greenish-yellow stamen), hasVisualFeature(Target, broad multi-petaled clematis-style flower). Wait no, more accurately, the target flower is the same type as the 81 labeled examples: the first two user images were pink/white clematis, 81 class. The target here is pale pink clematis flowers, so it's 81.

Wait let's formalize correctly:

<tbox>
- Class81 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen)
- hasVisualFeature(broad pink/white multi-petaled flower, prominent central stamen) sqsubseteq Class81
- Class14 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals)
- hasVisualFeature(bright yellow iris-shaped flower, narrow pointed petals) sqsubseteq Class14
</tbox>

<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, broad pale pink/white multi-petaled flower)
- hasVisualFeature(Target, prominent central yellow-green stamen)
</abox>

Then dl_explanation: The target image contains broad, multi-petaled flowers with pale pink and white coloring plus a prominent central stamen, which matches the necessary and sufficient visual features for Class81. The features do not match the yellow iris features of Class14, so the correct label is 81.

Then the final response is <response>81</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.
